perturbation.original_text: The Monthly Fees will be paid to us at the place and in the manner we designate on or before the fifteenth (15th) day of each month and will be accompanied by our standard schedule setting forth in reasonable detail the computation of the Monthly Fees for such month.
perturbation.changed_text: The Monthly Fees will be paid to us at the place and in the manner we designate on or before the last day of the month following the month for which the fees are due, and will be accompanied by our standard schedule setting forth in reasonable detail the computation of the Monthly Fees for such month.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to the end of the following month contradicts state-level wage and payment laws, which often require more frequent payment schedules.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut law mandates that employers pay wages at least once per week or bi-weekly. By setting the payment schedule to the end of the following month, this clause violates Connecticut's requirements for timely wage payments, making it a legal contradiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Connecticut General Statutes § 31-71b. (2022) - Payment of wages. Electronic direct deposit of wages for state employees. Exemptions. :: Title 31, Chapter 558 - Wages :: 2022 Connecticut General Statutes :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2023 Conn. Gen. Stat. 2022 Conn. Gen. Stat. (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Connecticut General Statutes Title 31 - Labor Chapter 558 - Wages Section 31-71b. - Payment of wages. Electronic direct deposit of wages for state employees. Exemptions. Universal Citation: CT Gen Stat § 31-71b. (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next (a)(1) Except as provided in subdivision (2) of this subsection, each employer, or the agent or representative of an employer, shall pay weekly, or once every two weeks, all wages, salary or other compensation due each employee on a regular pay day, designated in advance by the employer using one or more of the following methods: (A) Cash; (B) by negotiable checks; (C) upon an employee's written or electronic request, by direct deposit; or (D) by payroll card, provided the requirements of section 31-71k are satisfied. (2) Unless otherwise requested by the recipient, the Comptroller shall, as soon as is practicable, pay all wages due each state employee, as defined in section 5-196, by electronic direct deposit to such employee's account in any bank, Connecticut credit union or federal credit union that has agreed with the Comptroller to accept such wage deposits. (b) The end of the pay period for which payment is made on a regular pay day shall be not more than eight days before such regular pay day, provided, if such regular pay day falls on a nonwork day, payment shall be made on the preceding work day. (c) This section shall not be construed to (1) prohibit a local or regional board of education
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment deadline from "on or before the fifteenth (15th) day of each month" to "on or before the last day of the month following the month for which the fees are due." This contradicts Connecticut General Statutes § 31-71b, which mandates that employers "shall pay weekly, or once every two weeks, all wages, salary or other compensation due each employee on a regular pay day." The changed text extends the payment period beyond the legally permissible timeframe, establishing a clear violation of state wage laws as described in the provided snippet, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: During the Term and for two (2) years thereafter, we and our authorized agents have the right to verify Operational Information required under this Agreement by requesting, receiving, inspecting and auditing, at all reasonable times, any and all records referred to above wherever they may be located (or elsewhere if we request).
perturbation.changed_text: During the Term and for seven (7) years thereafter, we and our authorized agents have the right to verify Operational Information required under this Agreement by requesting, receiving, inspecting and auditing, at all reasonable times, any and all records referred to above wherever they may be located (or elsewhere if we request).
perturbation.explanation: Changing the audit period to seven years after the term may conflict with statutes of limitations on contract disputes or general business record retention requirements in Connecticut.
perturbation.law_explanation: Connecticut's statute of limitations for breach of contract is six years for written contracts. Extending the audit period to seven years post-termination allows audits beyond the period where legal action is viable, which could be deemed unreasonable or unenforceable. This is a legal contradiction because it extends rights beyond the legal recourse period.

perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the audit period to seven years, which contradicts the Connecticut statute of limitations for breach of written contracts, as the LLM Law explanation states it is six years. The original text has two years. The scraped law snippet 1 shows that Sec. 52-576 refers to actions on simple contracts. This section provides a list of different statutes of limitations for different causes of action. This means that the change to seven years creates a contradiction since legal action may no longer be viable beyond six years.
